coredata.dc:title: Covalent organic framework enhanced aggregation-induced emission of berberine and the application for detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tengfei
Wang, Liwen
Wu, Guang
Tian, Dating
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we prepared TaTp-COF, a covalent organic framework material with porous and uniform spheres, by hydrothermally reacting 1,3,5-tris (4-aminophenyl) benzene (TaPb) with terephthalaldehyde (TpTa). TaTp-COF could significantly enhance the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of berberine (BBR), a natural plant medicine, which was related to the porous structure, the suitable voids, and the hydrogen bonding between TaTp-COF and BBR that could limit the free movement of BBR. The unique emission properties of BBR on TaTp-COF were investigated and used for the sensitive detection of BBR based on the fluorescence intensity of AIE, with a linear range of 10–100 μ mol/L (R 2 = 0.9991) and a detection limit of 0.0453 μ;mol/L. Finally, the TaTp-COF was successfully applied to the detection of BBR in real samples, achieving good recoveries and satisfactory relative standard deviations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic framework (COF)
Aggregation-induced emission
Berberine
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of BBR in real samples
Fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence determination of BBR
Detection of BBR in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team for Excellent Young and Middle-aged in Higher Education Institutions
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dating Tian is a professor of chemistry at Hubei Minzu University, China. He received his Bachelor of Science degree from the Department of Chemistry at Central China Normal University in 1991. He obtained his Master degree and Ph.D. degree from the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 1997 and 2012, respectively. He has over 30 years of research experience in advanced functional materials and fluorescence probes. He has presided over and completed projects such as the National Natural Science Foundation of China, the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China, and the Hubei Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team for Excellent Young and Middle-aged in Higher Education Institutions. He is the author or co-author of over 60 peer-reviewed papers, two books, and one textbook.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)SEM and TEM(B) spectra of TaTp-COF; (C) FTIR spectra of TaPb (a), TpTa (b), and TaTp-COF(c); (D) XPS spectrum of TaTp-COF; High-resolution XPS spectra of (E) C1s and N1s (F) of TaTp-COF; (G) XRD patterns of TaTp-COF immersed in aqueous solution for various periods; (H) TG curve of TaTp-COF; (I) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of TaTp-COF and TaTp-COF+BBR.

(A) FL emission spectra of BBR, TaTp-COF, TaTp-COF@BBR under the same condition; (B) FL spectra of TaTp-COFs@BBR in the presence of different concentrations of BBR; (C) The linear relationship between the FL intensities of TaTp-COF@BBR with the concentrations of BBR; (D) Effects of different interfering substances on the FL intensities of TaTp-COF@BBR (from 1 to 20: K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, glycine, L-alanine, aspartic acid, glutathione, sodium sulfide, adenosine triphosphate, adenosine diphosphate, adenosine monophosphate, protopine, heme, and bilirubin).

(A) FTIR spectra of TaTp-COF(a), TaTp-COF@BBR(b), and BBR(c); (B) XPS spectrum of TaTp-COF@BBR; (C) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s; (D) High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s; (E) Three-dimensional size map of BBR; (F) FL intensities of TaPb+BBR (1), TpTa+BBR (2), ferric hydroxide colloid+BBR (3), and BBR (4).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis route of TaTp-COF and the detection of BBR.
Detection of BBR in berberine hydrochloride tablets and fetal bovine serum.

head.ce:title: Covalent organic framework enhanced aggregation-induced emission of berberine and the application for detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biological Resources Protection and Utilization, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Minzu University, Enshi 445000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biological Resources Protection and Utilization, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Minzu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biological Resources Protection and Utilization, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Minzu University, Enshi 445000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we prepared TaTp-COF, a covalent organic framework material with porous and uniform spheres, by hydrothermally reacting 1,3,5-tris (4-aminophenyl) benzene (TaPb) with terephthalaldehyde (TpTa). TaTp-COF could significantly enhance the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of berberine (BBR), a natural plant medicine, which was related to the porous structure, the suitable voids, and the hydrogen bonding between TaTp-COF and BBR that could limit the free movement of BBR. The unique emission properties of BBR on TaTp-COF were investigated and used for the sensitive detection of BBR based on the fluorescence intensity of AIE, with a linear range of 10–100 μ mol/L (R 2 = 0.9991) and a detection limit of 0.0453 μ;mol/L. Finally, the TaTp-COF was successfully applied to the detection of BBR in real samples, achieving good recoveries and satisfactory relative standard deviations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Berberine (BBR), an isoquinoline alkaloid isolated from Coptis chinensis, has a variety of biological properties, including antioxidant, antibacterial, antifungal, antiviral, anti-tumor, antidiarrhoeal, antidyslipidemic, and antidiabetic [1]. However, long-term or high-dose intake of BBR can lead to drug resistance and some serious side effects, such as heart failure, increased stomach acid, newborn jaundice, rapid cell apoptosis, and B vitamin absorption disorders [2,3]. Especially in recent years, the rising use of BBR in food production, animal husbandry, and aquaculture has drawn much attention, particularly in recent years, because it may endanger organism safety [4]. Currently, the primary methods for detecting BBR include liquid chromatography [5], capillary electrophoresis [6], electrochemical analysis [7], chemiluminescence [8], capillary electrophoresis coupled by laser-induced fluorescence detection [6], colorimetry [9], fluorescence spectrophotometry [10–12], and others. In contrast, fluorescence spectrophotometry has become an essential detection method due to its advantages of ease of use, low cost, excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity.

Traditional fluorescent molecules have substantial π-π stacking, which frequently results in aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), restricting their application [13]. Tang's group, on the other hand, discovered a type of molecule with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) in 2001 [14], which exhibits weak or no fluorescence in dispersed or dilute solutions but emits strong fluorescence in aggregated or solid states and has broader application prospect than traditional fluorescent molecules. Furthermore, Tang's group found that BBR exhibited a classic AIE effect [15]. As a result, as long as BBR's free mobility is suitably limited, the AIE effect of BBR can be significantly improved, allowing the detection of BBR by fluorescence method.

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are a novel type of porous organic materials linked by covalent bonds. COFs have numerous advantages, such as extensive pores, huge specific surface area, readily tunable skeleton, ultra-low density, good thermal stability, chemical stability, and so on. As a result, COF materials are widely used in a variety of applications, such as drug loading [16], catalysis [17,18], sensors [19,20], proton conduction [21], and energy storage [22]. As porous materials, COFs can connect to guest molecules by electrostatic interactions, π-π interactions, hydrogen bonding, and weak hydrogen bonding[23]. As a result, these interactions can be utilized to detect related substances. For example, Zhang's group reported the COF materials that selectively adsorbed anionic dyes through electrostatic interactions and have been applied to the detection of anionic dyes [24]. Song's group reported the use of COF as an adsorbent for solid-phase extraction of trace quantities of pesticide residues via π-π interactions before high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet detection [25]. Due to its porous structure and huge specific surface area, COF can provide favorable conditions for the binding of BBR, thereby limiting its free movement and improving its AIE effect, thus achieving the detection of BBR. As we know, traditional fluorescent detection methods for BBR generally rely on fluorescence turn-off [12,26]. Compared with the turn-off modes, the method based on fluorescence enhancement is more appealing for its higher sensitivity and superior anti-interference of background signal.

In this work, we synthesized a COF, TaTp-COF, using TaPb and TpTa and tested its performance to detect BBR. We found that TaTp-COF can effectively improve the fluorescence performance of BBR, mainly because BBR can effectively enter the cavity of TaTp-COF materials that limit the free movement of BBR through weak hydrogen bonding interactions, thereby inducing BBR to exhibit significant AIE effect. The resultant TaTp-COF exhibited little fluorescence effect in an aqueous solution, but when trace BBR was added, it emitted intense green fluorescence. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of the synthesis route of TaTp-COF and the detection of BBR. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that COF has been successfully applied to the detection of natural BBR with the AIE mechanism. Interestingly, we found that the specific detection of BBR can be achieved by the special emission wavelength. Finally, we successfully applied the TaTp-COF to the detection of BBR both in berberine hydrochloride tablets and in fetal bovine serum. In both actual sample detection processes, the TaTp-COF material showed good recovery rates and satisfactory RSD values.

Terephthalaldehyde(TpTa), 1,3,5-tris-(4-aminophenyl)benzene(TaPb), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), n-butanol, 1, 2-dichlorobenzene ethanol, acetic acid, berberine, pH standard buffer solution, fetal bovine serum, and other analytical chemicals were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The berberine hydrochloride tablets are provided by Northeast Pharmaceutical Group Shenyang Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiment was double distilled water.

Fluorescence (FL) spectra were taken on the Hitachi F-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), with an EX/EM slit width of 3.0/5.0 nm and a scanning speed of 1200 nm/min. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on the Thermo Nicolet 370 spectrometer, with a measurement range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy was performed on the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) ESCALAB 250 XI (UK). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size were measured by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (ASAP 2460, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using the Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer under conditions of 40 kV (voltage), 30 mA (current), and a scanning speed of 2°/min. The morphologies of the sample were obtained through transmission electron microscopy (TEM)(JOEL 2100-F, Tokyo, Japan) and field emission scanning electron microscopy(FE-SEM)(Tescan Mira 3 XH, Czech Republic). The absorption experiments were conducted on the TU-1901 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Beijing, China).

The synthesis of TaTp-COF was carried out based on literature with minor modifications [27]. Specifically, TaPb (0.16 mmol, 56.2 mg) and TpTa (0.24 mmol, 32.2 mg) were sonicated in the mixed solution of 1-butanol/1, 2-dichlorobenzene (3/3 mL) for 5 min, then acetic acid (6 mol/L, 0.6 mL) was added as a catalyst. Afterward, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave for reaction at 120 ℃ for 3 d. The crude product was refluxed through THF and EtOH sequentially to remove the trapped molecules, and then the TaTp-COF powder was collected by centrifugation and dried under vacuum overnight. Afterward, the TaTp-COF powder was dispersed in water at a concentration of 1 mg/mL to form a stock solution, which was then kept at 4 °C for future use.

Different amounts of BBR were mixed with 200 μL of TaTp-COF stock solution (1 mg/mL) to achieve final concentrations of 10–100 μmol/L. Then, the mixed solutions were ultrasonicated for 20 min and the FL intensities of different mixed solutions were measured using a 350 nm excitation wavelength at a 600 nm/min scanning rate. The FL response upon the interaction between TaTp-COF and BBR was carried out in optimal conditions.

Moreover, to investigate the specific FL response to BBR, the mixed solution of TaTp- COF with BBR was added with different interfering chemicals, such as K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, glycine, L-alanine, aspartic acid, glutathione, sodium sulfide, adenosine triphosphate, adenosine diphosphate, adenosine monophosphate, protopine, heme, and bilirubin. Under the same conditions, the FL intensities of the mixed solution were measured before and after the addition of interfering chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BBR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection of BBR in berberine hydrochloride tablets was performed as follows. Berberine hydrochloride tablets were firstly ground into uniform powders after removing the sugar coating. An appropriate amount of powder was accurately weighed and dissolved in 40 ℃ distilled water as the stock solution for further use. Then, different amounts of the stock solutions were mixed with the TaTp-COF solution. The FL intensities of different mixed solutions were measured using an FL spectrophotometer, and the concentration of BBR was calculated through a linear regression equation. The recovery rate was calculated according to Eq. 1 and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated through three parallel measurements under the same conditions.
(1)
Recovery =
Measured
concentration
Theoretical
concentration
× 100 %

The detection of BBR in fetal bovine serum was carried out as follows. To eliminate the interference of proteins in serum, the pre-treatment was performed on fetal bovine serum. Namely, 3 mL of trichloroacetic acid was added to 100 mL of fetal bovine serum to disrupt the activity of proteins in fetal bovine serum and the proteins were precipitated from the serum [12]. The processed serum was shaken vigorously for 15 min, centrifuged at a speed of 8000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was obtained. The above supernatant was further centrifuged twice, and the final supernatant was used to prepare the BBR stock solution. Then, different amounts of BBR stock solution were mixed with TaTp-COFs solution, and the FL intensities of different mixed solutions were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The concentration of BBR, the recovery rate, and RSD were calculated according to the previous method.

QY was obtained according to a previously published paper [28]. The data were recorded under the condition of 350 nm excitation wavelength and 3/5 nm slit width. The QY values were calculated based on Eq. 2 as follows.
(2)
Q Y =
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
2
R
× Q
Y
R
where I is the integral area of the FL spectrum, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the UV absorbance at 350 nm excitation wavelength, and R is the reference value.

All theoretical calculations were completed using the Gauss 09 program [29]. Gauss View software was used to build the initial structure, and then the B3LYP/6–31 G algorithm of density functional theory (DFT) was used to optimize the ground state structure of the compound [30,31].

We characterized the morphologies of the samples using SEM (Fig. 1A) and TEM (Fig. 1B and Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1A-1B and Fig. S1, the prepared samples were spherical porous materials with good dispersity, uniform structure, and an average particle size of approximately 120 nm. This can be explained by the fact that the two raw materials, TaPb and TpTa, first generate 2D TaTp-COFs materials, which can then be self-assembled into 3D spherical TaTp-COFs materials through π-π stacking[32]. The spherical surfaces of COF materials were relatively rough and there are abundant pores inside, which provide favorable conditions for subsequent adsorption of BBR.

Fig. 1C shows the FTIR spectra of TaPb (a), TpTa (b), and TaTp-COFs materials (c). As shown in Fig. 1C-a, the absorption peaks at 3432 cm−1 and 3355 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the N-H in TaPb. Fig. 1C-b shows the FTIR spectrum of TpTa. The absorption peak at 1689 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO, and that at 2861 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of C-H in TpTa. Fig. 1C-c shows the FTIR spectrum of TaTp-COF. A strong absorption peak appeared at 1619 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CN bond of the Schiff base formed by the reaction of amino with aldehyde groups [33,34]. The wide absorption peak at 3432 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of the N-H on the aromatic ring. The formation of stretching vibration of Schiff base groups and the disappearance of absorption peaks of amino and aldehyde indicated the successful preparation of TaTp-COF.

Fig. 1D shows the XPS spectrum of TaTp-COF. As shown in Fig. 1D, the peaks at 284.8 eV and 399.1 eV were attributed to the C and N, respectively. The peak at 532.2 eV might be caused by the unreacted aldehyde group [35]. Fig. 1E and Fig. 1F show the high-resolution XPS spectra of (E) C1s and N1s (F) of TaTp-COF, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1E, the C1s spectrum displayed two individual component peaks, corresponding to C of CN (285.2 eV) [35] and C-C/CC (284.64 eV) [36]. As shown in Fig. 1F, the N1s spectrum displayed three individual component peaks, corresponding to N of CN (401.6 eV), C-N (398.8 eV), and N-H (399.4 eV). The imine bonds (CN) were fully demonstrated by the analysis of XPS spectra of C1s and N1s, further proving the successful preparation of TaTp-COF.

Fig. 1G shows the XRD patterns of TaTp-COF immersed in an aqueous solution for various periods. The strength peak at 4.8° and the secondary strength peak at 7.3° belonged to the 110 and 210 crystal planes of TaTp-COF, respectively [37]. The weak peak at 25.7° was caused by the π-π interaction [38]. The XRD patterns of the prepared TaTp-COF were consistent with those reported in the previously published papers [30,39], indicating the successful preparation of TaTp-COF. To verify the reliability of TaTp-COF, we compared the XRD patterns of TaTp-COF immersed in an aqueous solution for various periods, as shown in Fig. 1G. The TaTp-COF could maintain a good crystal structure after being immersed in an aqueous solution for 10 d, demonstrating that it had good stability and could be used as a probe for detecting pollutants in aqueous solutions.

Fig. 1H shows the TG curve of TaTp-COF. As shown in Fig. 1H, during the 0–400 ℃ stage, the weight loss rate of the material was only 9 % as the temperature raised from 0 ℃ to 400 ℃, mainly corresponding to the volatilization of solvents and water inside the material. Due to the presence of the benzene ring and imine bond, TaTp- COF had a rigid structure and exhibited good thermal stability [40,41]. However, when the temperature rose to 800 ℃, the weight loss rate of TaTp-COF was as high as 45 %. This was because the framework of TaTp-COF was severely damaged under high temperatures [37].

Fig. 1I shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of TaTp-COF before and after adding BBR. As shown in Fig. 1I, the N2 adsorption-desorption curve of TaTp- COF was a type IV isotherm, indicating that the TaTp-COF was porous. The specific surface area of the TaTp-COF was 66.38 m2/g, while the TaTp-COF@BBR was 21.04 m2/g. The latter was significantly lower than the former, demonstrating the successful combination of BBR with TaTp-COF.

To study the AIE characteristics of BBR, the photoluminescence behavior of BBR in the H2O/THF mixture was studied. Fig. S2 shows the FL intensities of BBR in an H2O/THF mixture with different THF volume fractions. As shown in Fig. S2, BBR did not show significant luminescence performance in pure aqueous solution. However, the FL intensities of BBR were significantly enhanced with the increasing THF volume fractions in the H2O/THF mixture. Moreover, bright green light can also be observed under ultraviolet light (inset of Fig. S2). These results were mostly due to BBR gradually forming aggregates in poor solvents, which inhibited BBR's molecular movement and resulted in BBR exhibiting a fluorescence enhancement effect, i.e., BBR had an AIE effect [15]. Moreover, we investigated BBR's AIE characteristics through theoretical calculations (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. S3, simulation calculations revealed that there were C-H/O, C-H/C, C/O, and O/O interactions between adjacent BBR molecules in the H2O/THF mixture, with distances ranging from 2.6 to 3.1 Å, which could stiffen the molecular conformation[15]. Therefore, BBR molecules exhibited good fluorescence emission in poor solvents. As reported by the Tang′s group [15], the BBR molecule adopted a non-planar conformation in its crystal structure between the electron-donating phenyl group and the isoquinoline group, indicating the presence of intramolecular vibration behavior and twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) effect in BBR molecule. In a dilute solution or good solvent, the active intramolecular vibration and/or TICT effect could open a non-radiative decay pathway, resulting in weak emission. However, in aggregated state or poor solvent, the non-radiative decay was considerably suppressed, leading to strong emission. In other words, BBR had an AIE effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence determination of BBR
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. S4 shows the FL excitation and emission spectra of TaTp-COF@BBR. As shown in Fig. S4, the optimal excitation wavelength of TaTp-COF@BBR was 350 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 560 nm. Moreover, as the excitation wavelength changed from 330 nm to 370 nm, the emission peak of the TaTp-COF@BBR at 560 nm did not shift, indicating that TaTp-COF@BBR was not an excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectroscopy system [42]. We also investigated the effect of the incubation time of TaTp-COF with BBR on the FL intensities (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence signal was turned on immediately after the addition of BBR and the FL intensities tended to stabilize after 20 min. As a result, the incubation time for TaTp-COF and BBR in this investigation was set to 20 min. Additionally, Fig. S6 shows the effect of pH on the FL intensities of TaTp-COF@BBR. As shown in Fig. S6, the FL intensities of TaTp-COF@BBR were essentially unaltered in buffer solutions with varying pH levels. To avoid the impact of pH changes on the detection results, a buffer solution with pH= 7 was utilized in this investigation. Fig. 2A shows the FL emission spectra of BBR, TaTp-COF, and TaTp -COF@BBR under the same condition. As shown in Fig. 2A, the TaTp-COF did not emit fluorescence in an aqueous solution, which was useful for further detection. However, BBR emitted extremely weak fluorescence in an aqueous solution. Moreover, the FL intensities of BBR were significantly enhanced when BBR was mixed with TaTp-COF. These results enabled TaTp-COF can be used as an efficient fluorescence-enhanced probe to detect BBR. In addition, we investigated the UV–vis spectra of the samples. Fig. S7 shows the UV–vis spectra of TaTp-COF(a), BBR (b), and TaTp-COF@BBR (c). As shown in Fig. S7, the absorption peak of TaTp-COF@BBR at 365 nm was much higher than that of BBR, demonstrating that the addition of TaTp-COF might improve the UV–vis absorption ability of BBR. The result was in line with the previously published paper [43]. Fig. 2B shows the FL spectra of TaTp-COFs@BBR in the presence of different concentrations of BBR. As shown in Fig. 2B, as the concentration of BBR increased, the FL intensities of the TaTp-COF@BBR at 560 nm gradually increased. Fig. 3C shows the linear relationship between the FL intensities of TaTp-COF@BBR with the concentrations of BBR. A linear regression equation was obtained as F
i = 4.6387 C
i + 11.7744 (R 2 =0.9991), where F
i was the FL intensity of TaTp-COF@BBR and C
i was the concentration of BBR. The LOD (limit of detection) was determined as 0.0453 μ mol/L by the 3σ/Slope method (σ was the blank deviation). Fig. 2D shows the effect of different interfering substances on the FL intensities of TaTp-COF@BBR. As shown in Fig. 2D, the interfering substances did not interfere with the FL intensities of the TaTp-COF@BBR, which indicated that TaTp-COF can achieve specific detection of BBR. Moreover, we compared this method with other methods for detecting BBR used in the literature[12,26,44–46] (see Table S1). As shown in Table S1, this method had advantages such as low LOD and wide detection range. These findings suggested that TaTp-COF-enhanced AIE can be employed to detect BBR in the environment.

Based on the spectroscopic evidence presented above, we reasoned that BBR could only exhibit fluorescence amplification after assembling into the pore structure of TaTp-COF. To further examine the mechanism of TaTp-COF-enhanced AIE of BBR, we employed FTIR, XPS, DLS, and EDS to characterize the TaTp-COF@BBR. Fig. 3A shows the FTIR spectra of TaTp-COF(a), TaTp-COF@BBR (b), and BBR (c). As shown in Fig. 3A, the FTIR spectrum of TaTp-COF@BBR could be reviewed as the superposition pattern of TaTp-COF with BBR. Fig. 3B shows the XPS spectrum of TaTp-COF@BBR. By comparing Fig. 3B with Fig. 1D, it could be found that the peak intensity of O1s has been greatly enhanced. Fig. 3 C shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s. In a comparison of Fig. 3C with Fig. 1E, the peak appeared at 288.9 eV[47], corresponding to the CO. Fig. 3D shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum N1s. By comparing Fig. 3D with Fig. 1F, it could be seen that the peak areas (C-N and N-H) had decreased greatly, and the peak of CN at 402.1 eV shifted to a higher binding energy than that of CN at 401.6 eV in Fig. 1F. These results indicated that the addition of BBR significantly caused changes in the chemical environment in TaTp-COF. Fig. S8 shows the particle size distribution of TaTp-COF(A) and TaTp-COF@BBR (B). The average size of TaTp-COF was approximately 120 nm, while the average size of TaTp-COF after adding BBR was approximately 195 nm, indicating that BBR aggregated in the cavity of TaTp-COF. Fig. S9I and Fig. S9II show the EDS element mapping of TaTp-COF and TaTp-COF@BBR, respectively. By comparing Fig. S9I and Fig. S9II, it could be found that O content increased significantly and O dispersed uniformly in TaTp-COFs@BBR, showing that BBR has successfully entered and been fixed in the cavity of TaTp-COF. Fig. 3E shows the three-dimensional diagram of BBR. As shown in Fig. 3E, the three-dimensional size of BBR molecules calculated using the Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) method was 16.48 × 9.00 × 5.17 Å. According to the results of BET, the pore diameter of TaTp-COF was approximately 2.8 nm (Fig. S10), which was sufficient to accommodate BBR molecules. These characterizations validate the hypothesis that BBR could be assembled into the cavity of TaTp-COF. When BBR entered the pore cavity of TaTp-COF, the CN bond in TaTp-COF could interact with BBR through hydrogen bonding to fix BBR. Fig. 3F shows the FL intensities of TaPb mixed with BBR (1), TpTa mixed with BBR (2), ferric hydroxide colloid mixed BBR (3), and BBR (4). Under the same condition, neither TaPb nor TpTa could enhance the fluorescence of BBR, indicating that BBR responded only to the synthesized material rather than the residual reactants. Meanwhile, although ferric hydroxide colloid could absorb BBR, it did not enhance the fluorescence of BBR, further suggesting that the AIE of BBR only responded to TaTp-COF due to the specific physical structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BBR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the detection performance of TaTp-COF on BBR in actual samples, the contents of BBR in fetal bovine serum and berberine hydrochloride tablets were analyzed with TaTp-COF. Table 1 shows the detection results of BBR in fetal bovine serum and berberine hydrochloride tablets. As shown in Table 1, the recovery rates for fetal bovine serum ranged from 100.17 % and 103.25 %, while those for berberine hydrochloride tablets ranged from 96.92 % to 97.10 %, all with satisfactory RSD values. These results revealed that the use of TaTp-COF as an AIE-enhanced probe for detecting BBR was reliable.

In this study, we synthesized TaTp-COF material using TaPb and TpTa through the hydrothermal method. The synthesized TaTp-COF had rough surfaces and abundant pores, providing suitable sites for BBR (a natural plant medicine) assembly that was beneficial for improving the AIE performance of BBR. After sufficient characterization of TaTp-COF@BBR by various methods and theoretical calculations, we believed that the unique interaction between TaTp-COF and BBR resulted in BBR molecules being confined in the cavity of TaTp-COF, leading to the enhanced fluorescence of BBR. Moreover, a good linear relationship between the FL intensities of TaTp-COFs@BBR and the concentrations of BBR (10–100 μmol/L) was obtained, with a LOD of 0.0453 μ mol/L. Finally, the obtained TaTp-COF was applied to the detection of BBR in fetal bovine serum and berberine hydrochloride tablets, which also showed good recovery and satisfactory RSD values. These results indicated that the method of using TaTp-COF to enhance the AIE could reliably detect BBR in real samples. However, due to the relatively long preparation time of TaTp-COF, the detection cycle for the target analyte is extended, which should be further improved in future research.

Tengfei Wang: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Liwen Wang: Formal analysis, Validation. Dating Tian: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Guang Wu: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134867.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tengfei Wang received his Bachelor of Science degree in 2021 and is presently a graduate student at Hubei Minzu University, China. His study is focused on fluorescent probes.
Liwen Wang obtained her Bachelor of Engineering Science degree in 2022 and is presently a graduate student at Hubei Minzu University, China. His research interest is in fluorescent probes.
Guang Wu received his Bachelor of Engineering degree in 2021 and is now a graduate student at Hubei Minzu University, China. His study is focused on advanced functional materials.

Dating Tian is a professor of chemistry at Hubei Minzu University, China. He received his Bachelor of Science degree from the Department of Chemistry at Central China Normal University in 1991. He obtained his Master degree and Ph.D. degree from the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 1997 and 2012, respectively. He has over 30 years of research experience in advanced functional materials and fluorescence probes. He has presided over and completed projects such as the National Natural Science Foundation of China, the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China, and the Hubei Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team for Excellent Young and Middle-aged in Higher Education Institutions. He is the author or co-author of over 60 peer-reviewed papers, two books, and one textbook.
